informal: Prove that the ring $\mathbb{Z}\left[x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}, \ldots\right] /\left(x_{1} x_{2}, x_{3} x_{4}, x_{5} x_{6}, \ldots\right)$ contains infinitely many minimal prime ideals.
reasoning: The Lean signature defines f i = X(2i) * X(2i+1), so f 0 = X₀X₁, f 1 = X₂X₃, etc., matching the informal generators (x₁x₂, x₃x₄, ...) up to 0/1-indexing. The ambient ring MvPolynomial ℕ ℤ matches ℤ[x₁, x₂, …]. The quotient `MvPolynomial ℕ ℤ ⧸ span (range f)` requires `span` to resolve to `Ideal.span` (since ⧸ on a ring requires an ideal), which equals the ideal generated by these monomials. The conclusion `Infinite (minimalPrimes ...)` says the set/type of minimal primes is infinite, matching "infinitely many minimal prime ideals". The `hf` hypothesis just names f via a definitional equation, which doesn't change the mathematical content. No extra hypotheses are imposed and no quantifier structure is altered.
